u1_l1.1.title: Review of Algebra (factoring, radicals, rational expressions)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Review of Algebra (factoring, radicals, rational expressions)
summary_sections.content_html:
Review of Algebra

A solid algebra foundation is essential for Precalculus.

Factoring

  • GCF: Always factor out the greatest common factor first.
  • Difference of squares: a² − b² = (a + b)(a − b).
  • Perfect square trinomials: a² ± 2ab + b² = (a ± b)².
  • Trinomial factoring: ax² + bx + c — find factors of ac that add to b.
  • Grouping: For four-term polynomials, group pairs and factor each group.

Radicals

  • √(ab) = √a · √b; √(a/b) = √a/√b.
  • Rationalizing: Multiply by the conjugate to eliminate radicals from denominators.

Rational Expressions

  • Factor numerator and denominator, cancel common factors.
  • Add/subtract: Find LCD, rewrite fractions, combine.
  • Multiply/divide: Factor, cancel, and simplify.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Order of Operations & Exponents
summary_sections.content_html:
Order of Operations & Exponents

PEMDAS: Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication/Division (left→right) → Addition/Subtraction (left→right).

Exponent Rules

  • am · an = am+n
  • am/an = am−n
  • (am)n = amn
  • a⁰ = 1 (a ≠ 0)
  • a−n = 1/an
  • (ab)n = anbn
  • am/n = ⁿ√(am) = (ⁿ√a)m

These rules are used constantly throughout Precalculus and Calculus.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Relations vs. Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Relations vs. Functions

A relation is any set of ordered pairs (x, y). A function is a special relation where every input (x) has exactly one output (y).

  • Vertical Line Test: If any vertical line crosses the graph more than once, it's NOT a function.
  • Mapping diagrams: No input should have more than one arrow pointing to an output.
  • Function notation: f(x) means 'the output of function f for input x.'
  • One-to-one function: Each output corresponds to exactly one input. Passes the horizontal line test.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Domain & Range (interval notation)
summary_sections.content_html:
Domain & Range

The domain is the set of all valid inputs (x-values); the range is the set of all outputs (y-values).

Finding the Domain

  • Polynomials: Domain = (−∞, ∞).
  • Rational functions: Exclude values where the denominator = 0.
  • Square roots: The expression under the radical must be ≥ 0.
  • Logarithms: The argument must be > 0.

Interval Notation

  • ( ) = open (endpoint not included); [ ] = closed (included).
  • ∞ and −∞ always use parentheses.
  • Union (∪) combines disjoint intervals.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Function Notation & Evaluation
summary_sections.content_html:
Function Notation & Evaluation

Function notation f(x) names the function (f) and the input variable (x).

  • Evaluating: Replace x with the given value and simplify.
  • f(a + h): Substitute (a + h) for every x in the formula.
  • Difference quotient: [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h — fundamental to calculus (measures average rate of change).
  • Piecewise evaluation: Determine which piece applies based on the input value.
  • Reading function values from graphs: f(a) is the y-value where x = a on the graph.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphing Basics (intercepts, symmetry)
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Basics

Understanding graphs is fundamental to Precalculus.

Intercepts

  • x-intercept: Where y = 0. Set f(x) = 0 and solve for x.
  • y-intercept: Where x = 0. Evaluate f(0).

Symmetry

  • Even function: f(−x) = f(x). Symmetric about the y-axis. Example: f(x) = x².
  • Odd function: f(−x) = −f(x). Symmetric about the origin. Example: f(x) = x³.
  • Neither: Most functions are neither even nor odd.

Increasing/Decreasing

  • A function is increasing on an interval if f(a) < f(b) whenever a < b.
  • A function is decreasing on an interval if f(a) > f(b) whenever a < b.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Transformations (shifts, reflections, stretches)
summary_sections.content_html:
Transformations of Functions

Understanding transformations lets you graph functions by modifying parent functions.

Translations (Shifts)

  • f(x) + c: Shift UP c units.
  • f(x) − c: Shift DOWN c units.
  • f(x − c): Shift RIGHT c units.
  • f(x + c): Shift LEFT c units.

Reflections

  • −f(x): Reflect across the x-axis.
  • f(−x): Reflect across the y-axis.

Stretches & Compressions

  • a·f(x) with |a| > 1: Vertical stretch.
  • a·f(x) with 0 < |a| < 1: Vertical compression.
  • f(bx) with |b| > 1: Horizontal compression.
  • f(bx) with 0 < |b| < 1: Horizontal stretch.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Inverse Functions & Composition
summary_sections.content_html:
Inverse Functions & Composition

Composition

(f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)): Apply g first, then f to the result.

  • The domain of f ∘ g is restricted to x-values where g(x) is in the domain of f.

Inverse Functions

f⁻¹ undoes f: f(f⁻¹(x)) = x and f⁻¹(f(x)) = x.

  • Finding f⁻¹: Replace f(x) with y, swap x and y, solve for y.
  • Existence: Only one-to-one functions have inverses.
  • Graphically: The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f over the line y = x.
  • Domain of f⁻¹ = Range of f; Range of f⁻¹ = Domain of f.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Piecewise Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Piecewise Functions

A piecewise function uses different formulas for different parts of its domain.

  • Notation: f(x) = { formula₁ if condition₁; formula₂ if condition₂; … }.
  • Evaluating: Determine which condition the input satisfies, then use that formula.
  • Graphing: Graph each piece on its interval. Use open circles (○) for excluded endpoints, closed circles (●) for included endpoints.
  • Continuity: A piecewise function is continuous if the pieces connect without gaps or jumps.
  • Common examples: Absolute value f(x) = |x| = {x if x ≥ 0; −x if x < 0}; step functions; tax brackets.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Modeling
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Modeling

Functions model real-world situations by relating inputs to outputs.

  • Linear models: y = mx + b — constant rate of change. Example: distance = rate × time.
  • Quadratic models: Parabolic paths. Example: projectile motion h(t) = −16t² + v₀t + h₀.
  • Exponential models: Compound growth/decay. Example: population growth P(t) = P₀ert.
  • Piecewise models: Different rules for different conditions (tax brackets, tiered pricing).
  • Steps for modeling:
    1. Identify variables and relationships.
    2. Choose an appropriate function type.
    3. Write the equation.
    4. Solve and interpret in context.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Linear Equations & Graphs
summary_sections.content_html:
Linear Equations & Graphs

A linear equation in two variables has the form Ax + By = C (standard form) or y = mx + b (slope-intercept form).

  • Slope-intercept form: y = mx + b. m = slope, b = y-intercept.
  • Point-slope form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). Useful when you know a point and the slope.
  • Standard form: Ax + By = C. A, B, C are integers, A ≥ 0.
  • Slope: m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁) — the rate of change.
  • Horizontal line: y = c (slope = 0). Vertical line: x = c (slope undefined).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Slope, Intercepts, and Parallel/Perpendicular Lines
summary_sections.content_html:
Slope, Intercepts, and Parallel/Perpendicular Lines

Understanding slope relationships is key for many Precalculus topics.

  • Parallel lines: Same slope: m₁ = m₂.
  • Perpendicular lines: Slopes are negative reciprocals: m₁ · m₂ = −1.
  • Finding intercepts: x-intercept: set y = 0. y-intercept: set x = 0.
  • Slope from standard form: Ax + By = C → m = −A/B.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Quadratic Functions (vertex form, standard form)
summary_sections.content_html:
Quadratic Functions

A quadratic function has the form f(x) = ax² + bx + c (standard form) or f(x) = a(x − h)² + k (vertex form).

  • Standard form: f(x) = ax² + bx + c. Vertex at x = −b/(2a).
  • Vertex form: f(x) = a(x − h)² + k. Vertex at (h, k).
  • Direction: a > 0 → opens up (minimum); a < 0 → opens down (maximum).
  • Axis of symmetry: x = h (vertical line through the vertex).
  • Converting: Complete the square to go from standard to vertex form.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Completing the Square
summary_sections.content_html:
Completing the Square

Completing the square converts ax² + bx + c into a(x − h)² + k form.

Steps

  1. Factor out a from the x² and x terms: a(x² + (b/a)x) + c.
  2. Take half of the coefficient of x: (b/(2a)).
  3. Square it: (b/(2a))².
  4. Add and subtract this value inside the parentheses.
  5. Factor the perfect square trinomial and simplify.

Applications: Converting to vertex form, deriving the quadratic formula, graphing parabolas, and solving equations.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Quadratic Formula & Complex Solutions
summary_sections.content_html:
Quadratic Formula & Complex Solutions

Quadratic Formula: x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / (2a)

Discriminant: Δ = b² − 4ac

  • Δ > 0: Two distinct real solutions.
  • Δ = 0: One repeated real solution.
  • Δ < 0: Two complex (imaginary) solutions.

Complex Numbers

  • i = √(−1); i² = −1.
  • Complex solutions come in conjugate pairs: a + bi and a − bi.
  • Example: x² + 1 = 0 → x = ±i.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphing Quadratic Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Quadratic Functions

To graph a parabola, identify key features:

  1. Vertex: (h, k) from vertex form or x = −b/(2a).
  2. Direction: a > 0 (up), a < 0 (down).
  3. Axis of symmetry: x = h.
  4. y-intercept: f(0) = c.
  5. x-intercepts: Solve f(x) = 0 (use factoring, quadratic formula, or completing the square).
  6. Additional points: Choose x-values near the vertex and use symmetry.

The focus and directrix of a parabola play a role in conic sections (covered in later units).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications of Linear & Quadratic Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications of Linear & Quadratic Models

Linear and quadratic functions model many real-world situations.

  • Linear: Constant rate of change — distance-rate-time, cost functions, depreciation.
  • Quadratic: Parabolic behavior — projectile motion, area optimization, revenue/profit models.
  • Revenue: R(x) = (price)(quantity) = (p₀ − mx)x = p₀x − mx² — quadratic.
  • Profit: P(x) = R(x) − C(x).
  • Optimization: Find the vertex to maximize/minimize quadratic models.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Absolute Value Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Absolute Value Functions

f(x) = |x| creates a V-shape. The general form is f(x) = a|x − h| + k.

  • Vertex: (h, k).
  • Opening: a > 0 opens up (V); a < 0 opens down (inverted V).
  • Slope of the arms: +a on the right side, −a on the left side.
  • Solving |x| = c: If c > 0: x = c or x = −c. If c = 0: x = 0. If c < 0: no solution.
  • Solving |expression| < c: −c < expression < c (compound inequality).
  • Solving |expression| > c: expression > c OR expression < −c.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Inequalities (linear & quadratic)
summary_sections.content_html:
Inequalities

Linear Inequalities

  • Solve like equations. Flip the inequality when multiplying/dividing by a negative.
  • Graph on a number line: open circle for < or >, closed for ≤ or ≥.

Quadratic Inequalities

  1. Solve the equality ax² + bx + c = 0 to find critical values.
  2. Test intervals between and beyond the critical values.
  3. Determine which intervals satisfy the inequality.

Sign chart method: Factor the quadratic, determine sign of each factor in each interval, combine.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Characteristics of Polynomials (degree, leading coefficient)
summary_sections.content_html:
Characteristics of Polynomials

A polynomial function has the form f(x) = aₙxⁿ + aₙ₋₁xⁿ⁻¹ + … + a₁x + a₀, where the exponents are non-negative integers and the coefficients are real numbers.

Key Vocabulary

  • Degree: The highest exponent. It determines the overall shape and maximum number of turning points (≤ n − 1).
  • Leading coefficient (aₙ): The coefficient of the highest-degree term. Its sign determines end behavior.
  • Constant term (a₀): The y-intercept when x = 0.

Classification by Degree

  • Degree 0 → constant, Degree 1 → linear, Degree 2 → quadratic, Degree 3 → cubic, Degree 4 → quartic, Degree 5 → quintic.

Number of Zeros

  • A polynomial of degree n has at most n real zeros (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: exactly n zeros counting multiplicity over the complex numbers).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: End Behavior & Graph Shapes
summary_sections.content_html:
End Behavior & Graph Shapes

End behavior describes what happens to f(x) as x → +∞ and x → −∞. It depends on the degree and the sign of the leading coefficient.

Rules

  • Even degree, positive leading coeff: ↑ left and ↑ right (both ends rise).
  • Even degree, negative leading coeff: ↓ left and ↓ right (both ends fall).
  • Odd degree, positive leading coeff: ↓ left and ↑ right.
  • Odd degree, negative leading coeff: ↑ left and ↓ right.

Graph Shape Clues

  • Degree 2 (quadratic): parabola shape.
  • Degree 3 (cubic): S-shaped curve.
  • Degree 4 (quartic): W- or M-shaped (up to 3 turning points).
  • The number of turning points is at most n − 1.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Factoring Techniques (synthetic division, long division)
summary_sections.content_html:
Factoring Techniques

Dividing polynomials is essential for finding factors and zeros of higher-degree polynomials.

Polynomial Long Division

  • Works exactly like numerical long division.
  • Divide the leading term of the dividend by the leading term of the divisor, multiply, subtract, bring down, and repeat.
  • Result: f(x) = d(x)·q(x) + r(x), where r has lower degree than d.

Synthetic Division

  • A shortcut when dividing by (x − c).
  • Use only the coefficients; bring down, multiply by c, add, repeat.
  • The last number is the remainder (also equals f(c) by the Remainder Theorem).

Remainder & Factor Theorems

  • Remainder Theorem: When f(x) is divided by (x − c), the remainder is f(c).
  • Factor Theorem: (x − c) is a factor of f(x) if and only if f(c) = 0.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Roots, Zeros, and Multiplicity
summary_sections.content_html:
Roots, Zeros, and Multiplicity

The zeros (roots) of a polynomial f(x) are the x-values where f(x) = 0. They correspond to x-intercepts of the graph.

Multiplicity

  • If (x − c)ᵏ is a factor and k ≥ 1, then c is a zero of multiplicity k.
  • Odd multiplicity: the graph crosses the x-axis at x = c.
  • Even multiplicity: the graph touches (bounces off) the x-axis at x = c.

Finding Zeros

  • Factor the polynomial completely.
  • Set each factor equal to zero.
  • Use the quadratic formula for irreducible quadratic factors.

Writing a Polynomial from Zeros

  • If zeros are c₁, c₂, …, cₙ, then f(x) = a(x − c₁)(x − c₂)…(x − cₙ).
  • Use a given point to find the leading coefficient a.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Intermediate Value Theorem
summary_sections.content_html:
Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)

The Intermediate Value Theorem states: if f is continuous on [a, b] and N is any number between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f(c) = N.

Key Points

  • All polynomials are continuous everywhere, so IVT always applies to polynomials.
  • Most commonly used with N = 0 to guarantee the existence of a real zero between a and b when f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs.

Applying the IVT

  1. Evaluate f(a) and f(b).
  2. If f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, the IVT guarantees at least one zero in (a, b).
  3. The theorem does NOT tell you the exact value of the zero—only that it exists.

Limitations

  • IVT requires continuity; it does not apply to functions with discontinuities on [a, b].
  • A sign change is sufficient but not necessary—a zero can exist even without a sign change (e.g., touching the axis).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphing Polynomial Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Polynomial Functions

To sketch the graph of a polynomial, combine what you know about zeros, multiplicity, end behavior, and turning points.

Step-by-Step Graphing

  1. Find the y-intercept: evaluate f(0).
  2. Find the zeros: factor and solve f(x) = 0. Note multiplicity.
  3. Determine end behavior: use degree and leading coefficient.
  4. Plot key points: vertex (for quadratics), turning points, intercepts.
  5. Sketch: connect points smoothly, respecting multiplicity behavior (cross or bounce).

Using Technology

  • Graphing calculators or software can verify your sketch.
  • Use the table feature to find approximate turning-point coordinates.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Rational Root Theorem
summary_sections.content_html:
Rational Root Theorem

The Rational Root Theorem helps find possible rational zeros of a polynomial with integer coefficients.

Statement

  • If f(x) = aₙxⁿ + … + a₀ has integer coefficients, then any rational zero p/q (in lowest terms) satisfies:
  • p divides a₀ (the constant term) and q divides aₙ (the leading coefficient).

Using the Theorem

  1. List all factors of the constant term (±).
  2. List all factors of the leading coefficient (±).
  3. Form all possible fractions p/q.
  4. Test each candidate using direct substitution or synthetic division.
  5. Once a zero is found, factor it out and repeat with the reduced polynomial.

Limitations

  • Only identifies possible rational zeros; irrational and complex zeros are not found this way.
  • Not all candidates will be actual zeros—you must test them.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications of Polynomial Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications of Polynomial Models

Polynomials model many real-world phenomena: volumes, areas, projectile paths, revenue/cost functions, and data fitting.

Common Applications

  • Volume problems: Building an open-top box by cutting squares from a sheet → cubic model.
  • Revenue/Profit: R(x) or P(x) often modeled by quadratic or higher-degree polynomials.
  • Curve fitting: Using data points to determine a polynomial model (regression).

Setting Up a Model

  1. Define the variable and its realistic domain.
  2. Write the polynomial expression relating the quantities.
  3. Find the desired quantity: maximum, minimum, zero, or specific value.

Interpreting Results

  • Always check that answers fall within the practical domain (e.g., lengths must be positive).
  • Zeros represent break-even points; the vertex (for quadratics) gives max/min; critical points (for higher degrees) need calculus or technology to find.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Economics & Physics
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Economics & Physics

Polynomials appear naturally in both economics and physics. This lesson explores real-world examples that connect polynomial theory to practice.

Economics Applications

  • Cost functions: C(x) = aₙxⁿ + … + a₁x + a₀ models total cost of producing x units.
  • Demand & Revenue: When price depends linearly on quantity, revenue is quadratic. Higher-order demand models yield cubic or quartic revenue.
  • Marginal cost/revenue: The derivative of cost or revenue, foreshadowing calculus.

Physics Applications

  • Kinematics: Position as a polynomial in time: s(t) = s₀ + v₀t + ½at² (quadratic for constant acceleration).
  • Work & Energy: Work done by a variable force can involve polynomial integration.
  • Optics: Polynomial approximations (e.g., Snell's law corrections) appear in lens design.

Strategy

  • Identify the polynomial model, determine the domain, and use zeros, vertices, or technology to answer the question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Rational Expressions & Simplification
summary_sections.content_html:
Rational Expressions & Simplification

A rational expression is a ratio of two polynomials: P(x)/Q(x), where Q(x) ≠ 0.

Simplifying

  • Factor both the numerator and denominator completely.
  • Cancel common factors (noting excluded values where Q = 0).

Operations

  • Multiply: Factor, cancel, then multiply remaining numerators and denominators.
  • Divide: Multiply by the reciprocal of the divisor.
  • Add/Subtract: Find the LCD, rewrite each fraction, then combine numerators.

Complex Fractions

  • A fraction within a fraction. Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by the LCD of all mini-fractions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Vertical, Horizontal, and Oblique Asymptotes
summary_sections.content_html:
Vertical, Horizontal, and Oblique Asymptotes

Asymptotes are lines that a graph approaches but typically never reaches.

Vertical Asymptotes (VA)

  • Occur where the denominator = 0 and the numerator ≠ 0 (after simplification).
  • The graph goes to ±∞ near a VA.

Horizontal Asymptotes (HA)

  • Compare degrees of numerator (n) and denominator (m):
  • n < m → HA is y = 0.
  • n = m → HA is y = (leading coeff of num)/(leading coeff of den).
  • n > m → no horizontal asymptote.

Oblique (Slant) Asymptotes

  • Occur when n = m + 1 (degree of numerator is exactly one more than denominator).
  • Perform polynomial long division; the quotient (ignoring remainder) is the oblique asymptote.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Holes and Discontinuities
summary_sections.content_html:
Holes and Discontinuities

When both numerator and denominator share a common factor, canceling it creates a hole (removable discontinuity) rather than a vertical asymptote.

Identifying Holes

  • Factor numerator and denominator.
  • A common factor (x − c) that cancels creates a hole at x = c.
  • The y-coordinate of the hole is found by substituting c into the simplified expression.

Types of Discontinuities

  • Removable (hole): Factor cancels; the limit exists but the function is undefined there.
  • Non-removable (VA): The function → ±∞; the limit does not exist as a finite number.
  • Jump: Occurs in piecewise functions where left and right limits differ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphing Rational Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Rational Functions

Combine asymptotes, intercepts, holes, and sign analysis to sketch rational function graphs.

Steps

  1. Factor numerator and denominator; identify holes.
  2. Find vertical asymptotes (remaining denominator zeros).
  3. Find the horizontal or oblique asymptote.
  4. Find x-intercepts (numerator = 0) and y-intercept (x = 0).
  5. Use a sign chart to determine where f is positive or negative.
  6. Plot key points and sketch.

Additional Tips

  • Check symmetry: f(−x) = f(x) → even symmetry, f(−x) = −f(x) → odd symmetry.
  • The graph approaches but typically doesn't cross a VA; it may cross a HA.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Partial Fraction Decomposition
summary_sections.content_html:
Partial Fraction Decomposition

Partial fraction decomposition reverses the process of combining fractions: it breaks a single rational expression into a sum of simpler fractions.

When to Use

  • The degree of the numerator must be less than the degree of the denominator. (If not, perform long division first.)

Cases

  • Distinct linear factors: A/(x − a) + B/(x − b) + …
  • Repeated linear factor: (x − a)² → A/(x − a) + B/(x − a)².
  • Irreducible quadratic factor: (ax² + bx + c) → (Ax + B)/(ax² + bx + c).

Solving for Coefficients

  • Multiply both sides by the LCD, then equate coefficients or substitute convenient x-values.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Real-World Ratios (rates, proportions)
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Real-World Ratios

Rational functions model situations involving rates, concentrations, averages, and work problems.

Rate Problems

  • Average speed = total distance / total time. If parts of a trip have different speeds, the overall average is a rational expression.

Mixture / Concentration

  • C(x) = (initial amount + added amount) / (initial volume + added volume).

Work Problems

  • If A takes a hours and B takes b hours to complete a job alone, together: 1/a + 1/b = 1/t → t = ab/(a+b).

Average Cost

  • C̄(x) = C(x)/x. As production increases, fixed costs are spread over more units, reducing average cost (up to a point).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Rational Inequalities
summary_sections.content_html:
Rational Inequalities

Solving inequalities involving rational expressions requires sign analysis rather than simple algebraic manipulation.

Steps

  1. Move all terms to one side so that 0 is on the other: f(x)/g(x) ≤ 0 (or >, ≥, <).
  2. Factor numerator and denominator; find critical numbers (zeros of numerator and denominator).
  3. Create a sign chart on a number line using the critical numbers.
  4. Test a value in each interval to determine the sign of the expression.
  5. Select the intervals that satisfy the inequality.
  6. Include endpoints where the expression = 0 (for ≤ or ≥) but exclude where denominator = 0.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Engineering
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Engineering

Rational functions appear in engineering contexts such as electrical circuits, fluid dynamics, and structural design.

Electrical Circuits

  • Parallel resistors: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ → R_total = R₁R₂/(R₁ + R₂).
  • Transfer functions in control systems are ratios of polynomials.

Fluid Dynamics

  • Flow rate through a pipe may depend on pressure ratios modeled by rational expressions.

Structural Engineering

  • Load distribution and stress-strain relationships can involve rational models.
  • Safety factor = strength/stress is a ratio subject to constraints.

Optimization

  • Minimize material while meeting strength requirements → rational function optimization.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Exponential Growth & Decay Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Exponential Growth & Decay Models

An exponential function has the form f(x) = a · bˣ, where a ≠ 0 and b > 0, b ≠ 1.

Growth vs. Decay

  • Growth: b > 1. The function increases rapidly.
  • Decay: 0 < b < 1. The function decreases toward zero.

Natural Exponential

  • f(x) = eˣ, where e ≈ 2.71828 (Euler's number).
  • Continuous model: A(t) = A₀ · eᵏᵗ, k > 0 for growth, k < 0 for decay.

Half-Life & Doubling Time

  • Half-life: time for quantity to halve. A(t) = A₀ · (1/2)^(t/h).
  • Doubling time: time for quantity to double. 2 = e^(k·T_d) → T_d = ln 2 / k.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Compound Interest & Continuous Growth
summary_sections.content_html:
Compound Interest & Continuous Growth

Compound Interest

  • A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compoundings per year, t = years.
  • More frequent compounding yields more interest.

Continuous Compounding

  • As n → ∞: A = Peʳᵗ.
  • This is the maximum possible growth for a given rate r.

APY (Annual Percentage Yield)

  • Effective annual rate: APY = (1 + r/n)ⁿ − 1.
  • For continuous compounding: APY = eʳ − 1.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Properties of Logarithms
summary_sections.content_html:
Properties of Logarithms

A logarithm is the inverse of an exponential: if bˣ = y, then log_b(y) = x.

Key Properties

  • Product Rule: log_b(MN) = log_b(M) + log_b(N).
  • Quotient Rule: log_b(M/N) = log_b(M) − log_b(N).
  • Power Rule: log_b(Mⁿ) = n · log_b(M).
  • log_b(1) = 0 and log_b(b) = 1.
  • b^(log_b(x)) = x and log_b(bˣ) = x (inverse properties).

Common & Natural Logarithms

  • Common: log(x) = log₁₀(x).
  • Natural: ln(x) = logₑ(x).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Change of Base Formula
summary_sections.content_html:
Change of Base Formula

Calculators typically only have log (base 10) and ln (base e). To evaluate log_b(x) for other bases, use the change of base formula.

Formula

  • log_b(x) = log(x) / log(b) = ln(x) / ln(b).
  • More generally: log_b(x) = log_a(x) / log_a(b) for any valid base a.

Applications

  • Evaluate log₅(20) on a calculator: ln(20)/ln(5).
  • Compare logarithms in different bases.
  • Solve equations involving different bases.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solving Exponential & Logarithmic Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Exponential & Logarithmic Equations

Exponential Equations

  • If same base: bˣ = bʸ → x = y.
  • Otherwise: take log or ln of both sides → x · ln(b) = ln(result) → solve for x.

Logarithmic Equations

  • Isolate the log expression, then convert to exponential form.
  • Combine logs using properties first, if needed.

Extraneous Solutions

  • Always check solutions in the original equation.
  • Reject any solution that makes a log argument ≤ 0.

u5_l5.6.title: Applications: pH, Richter Scale, Population Growth

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications: pH, Richter Scale, Population Growth
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications: pH, Richter Scale, Population Growth

pH Scale

  • pH = −log[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the hydrogen ion concentration.
  • pH < 7: acidic; pH = 7: neutral; pH > 7: basic.

Richter Scale

  • M = log(I/I₀), where I is the intensity and I₀ is a reference.
  • Each whole number increase represents a 10× increase in amplitude.

Decibels

  • dB = 10 log(I/I₀). A 10 dB increase means 10× the intensity.

Population Growth

  • P(t) = P₀ · eᵏᵗ (exponential model).
  • Use ln to solve for t given a target population.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphing Exponential & Logarithmic Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Exponential & Logarithmic Functions

Exponential Graphs y = bˣ

  • Passes through (0, 1). HA: y = 0.
  • b > 1: increasing; 0 < b < 1: decreasing.

Transformations

  • y = a · b^(x − h) + k shifts the graph right h, up k, and stretches vertically by a.
  • HA shifts to y = k.

Logarithmic Graphs y = log_b(x)

  • Passes through (1, 0). VA: x = 0.
  • Reflection of y = bˣ over the line y = x.
  • b > 1: increasing; 0 < b < 1: decreasing.

Transformations of Log

  • y = a · log_b(x − h) + k: VA shifts to x = h.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Logistic Growth Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Logistic Growth Models

The logistic model accounts for a carrying capacity that limits exponential growth.

Formula

  • P(t) = L / (1 + be⁻ᵏᵗ), where L = carrying capacity, b and k are positive constants.

Characteristics

  • S-shaped (sigmoid) curve.
  • Initially grows roughly exponentially.
  • Growth rate slows as P approaches L.
  • Horizontal asymptotes: y = 0 (as t → −∞) and y = L (as t → ∞).
  • Inflection point at P = L/2 (fastest growth occurs here).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Biology & Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Biology & Finance

Exponential and logarithmic functions are widely used in both biological and financial modeling.

Biology

  • Bacterial growth: N(t) = N₀ · 2^(t/d) where d = doubling time.
  • Drug elimination: C(t) = C₀ · e⁻ᵏᵗ (first-order kinetics).
  • Carbon dating: A(t) = A₀ · e^(−0.000121t).

Finance

  • Compound interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt).
  • Present value: P = A · e⁻ʳᵗ (continuous discounting).
  • Depreciation: V(t) = V₀(1 − r)ᵗ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Angles in Degrees & Radians
summary_sections.content_html:
Angles in Degrees & Radians

Angles can be measured in degrees or radians.

Conversions

  • 360° = 2π radians; 180° = π radians.
  • Degrees → radians: multiply by π/180.
  • Radians → degrees: multiply by 180/π.

Standard Position

  • Vertex at origin, initial side along positive x-axis.
  • Positive angles: counter-clockwise. Negative angles: clockwise.

Quadrants

  • Q I: 0°–90°; Q II: 90°–180°; Q III: 180°–270°; Q IV: 270°–360°.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Arc Length & Sector Area
summary_sections.content_html:
Arc Length & Sector Area

Arc Length

  • s = rθ, where r = radius and θ is in radians.

Sector Area

  • A = (1/2)r²θ, where θ is in radians.

Linear & Angular Speed

  • Angular speed: ω = θ/t (rad/s).
  • Linear speed: v = rω = s/t.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Unit Circle & Special Angles
summary_sections.content_html:
Unit Circle & Special Angles

The unit circle has radius 1 centered at the origin. A point on it at angle θ has coordinates (cos θ, sin θ).

Special Angles

  • 0° (0): (1, 0)
  • 30° (π/6): (√3/2, 1/2)
  • 45° (π/4): (√2/2, √2/2)
  • 60° (π/3): (1/2, √3/2)
  • 90° (π/2): (0, 1)

Symmetry

  • Use reference angles and quadrant signs to extend to all angles.
  • ASTC: All, Sine, Tangent, Cosine positive in Q I, II, III, IV.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sine, Cosine, Tangent Definitions
summary_sections.content_html:
Sine, Cosine, Tangent Definitions

Right Triangle Definitions

  • sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse
  • cos θ = adjacent / hypotenuse
  • tan θ = opposite / adjacent = sin θ / cos θ

Reciprocal Functions

  • csc θ = 1/sin θ = hyp/opp
  • sec θ = 1/cos θ = hyp/adj
  • cot θ = 1/tan θ = adj/opp = cos θ / sin θ

Pythagorean Identity

  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1

u6_l6.5.title: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions (amplitude, period, phase shift)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions (amplitude, period, phase shift)
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphs of Trigonometric Functions

y = A sin(Bx − C) + D

  • Amplitude = |A|: vertical stretch.
  • Period = 2π/|B|: horizontal length of one cycle.
  • Phase shift = C/B: horizontal shift.
  • Vertical shift = D: midline moves to y = D.

Key Graphs

  • sin x: starts at 0, period 2π.
  • cos x: starts at 1, period 2π.
  • tan x: period π, vertical asymptotes at π/2 + nπ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Inverse trig functions find the angle given a ratio.

Definitions & Ranges

  • sin⁻¹(x) or arcsin(x): domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2].
  • cos⁻¹(x) or arccos(x): domain [−1, 1], range [0, π].
  • tan⁻¹(x) or arctan(x): domain (−∞, ∞), range (−π/2, π/2).

Key Relationships

  • sin(sin⁻¹(x)) = x for x ∈ [−1, 1].
  • sin⁻¹(sin θ) = θ only if θ ∈ [−π/2, π/2].

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Physics & Engineering
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Physics & Engineering

Simple Harmonic Motion

  • x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ) models oscillation (springs, pendulums).
  • A = amplitude, ω = angular frequency, φ = phase, period T = 2π/ω.

Waves

  • y = A sin(kx − ωt): traveling wave. k = 2π/λ (wave number), λ = wavelength.

AC Circuits

  • V(t) = V₀ sin(2πft + φ). f = frequency (Hz), peak voltage V₀.

Projectile Motion

  • Horizontal: x = v₀ cos θ · t. Vertical: y = v₀ sin θ · t − (1/2)gt².

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Coterminal & Reference Angles
summary_sections.content_html:
Coterminal & Reference Angles

Coterminal Angles

  • Two angles are coterminal if they share the same terminal side.
  • Add or subtract 360° (or 2π) to find coterminal angles.

Reference Angles

  • The reference angle is the positive acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis.
  • Q I: ref = θ; Q II: ref = 180° − θ; Q III: ref = θ − 180°; Q IV: ref = 360° − θ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Navigation
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Navigation

Trigonometry is fundamental to navigation on land, sea, and air.

Bearings

  • A bearing is measured clockwise from due north: N 30° E means 30° east of north.

Right-Triangle Navigation

  • Use trig ratios to find distances and angles in navigation problems.

Law of Sines & Cosines (preview)

  • Law of Sines: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C.
  • Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C.

GPS & Triangulation

  • GPS uses at least 3 satellites and trilateration (related to triangulation) to determine position.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Pythagorean Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Pythagorean Identities

The three Pythagorean identities are derived from sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

The Three Forms

  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1
  • 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ (divide by cos²θ)
  • 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ (divide by sin²θ)

Using Identities

  • Simplify expressions by substituting one identity for another.
  • Verify identities by transforming one side to match the other.
  • Rewrite in terms of sine and cosine as a common strategy.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sum & Difference Formulas
summary_sections.content_html:
Sum & Difference Formulas

Sine

  • sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B
  • sin(A − B) = sin A cos B − cos A sin B

Cosine

  • cos(A + B) = cos A cos B − sin A sin B
  • cos(A − B) = cos A cos B + sin A sin B

Tangent

  • tan(A + B) = (tan A + tan B)/(1 − tan A tan B)
  • tan(A − B) = (tan A − tan B)/(1 + tan A tan B)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Double-Angle & Half-Angle Formulas
summary_sections.content_html:
Double-Angle & Half-Angle Formulas

Double-Angle

  • sin 2θ = 2 sin θ cos θ
  • cos 2θ = cos²θ − sin²θ = 2cos²θ − 1 = 1 − 2sin²θ
  • tan 2θ = 2 tan θ / (1 − tan²θ)

Half-Angle

  • sin(θ/2) = ±√((1 − cos θ)/2)
  • cos(θ/2) = ±√((1 + cos θ)/2)
  • tan(θ/2) = sin θ/(1 + cos θ) = (1 − cos θ)/sin θ
  • Sign (±) depends on the quadrant of θ/2.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Product-to-Sum and Sum-to-Product Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Product-to-Sum and Sum-to-Product Identities

Product-to-Sum

  • sin A cos B = (1/2)[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)]
  • cos A cos B = (1/2)[cos(A−B) + cos(A+B)]
  • sin A sin B = (1/2)[cos(A−B) − cos(A+B)]

Sum-to-Product

  • sin C + sin D = 2 sin((C+D)/2) cos((C−D)/2)
  • sin C − sin D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) sin((C−D)/2)
  • cos C + cos D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) cos((C−D)/2)
  • cos C − cos D = −2 sin((C+D)/2) sin((C−D)/2)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solving Trigonometric Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Trigonometric Equations

Strategy

  • Isolate the trig function (use identities if needed).
  • Find reference angle solutions.
  • Use the unit circle to find all solutions in [0, 2π).
  • Add the period (2πn for sin/cos, πn for tan) for general solutions.

Quadratic-Type Equations

  • Substitute u = sin θ or cos θ, solve the quadratic, then back-substitute.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Modeling Periodic Phenomena
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Modeling Periodic Phenomena

Trigonometric functions model any quantity that repeats at regular intervals.

Examples

  • Temperature: T(t) = A sin(B(t − C)) + D models daily/yearly temperature cycles.
  • Tides: Height varies sinusoidally with period ≈ 12.4 hours.
  • Sound: Pressure waves modeled by sine functions; frequency = pitch.
  • Daylight hours: Varies sinusoidally over a year.

Fitting a Sinusoidal Model

  • Amplitude = (max − min)/2
  • Vertical shift D = (max + min)/2
  • Period = distance between repeating patterns → B = 2π/period.
  • Phase shift: adjust C to align with data.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Engineering
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Engineering

Trig identities and equations are essential in signal processing, structural analysis, and control systems.

Signal Processing

  • Fourier analysis decomposes signals into sums of sine and cosine functions.
  • Noise filtering uses trig identities to isolate frequencies.

Structural Analysis

  • Force resolution: F_x = F cos θ, F_y = F sin θ.
  • Stress analysis in beams uses trig for angle-dependent loads.

Control Systems

  • Phase margin and gain involve trig relationships.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Calculus Prep
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Calculus Prep: Trigonometric Skills

Calculus builds heavily on trig. These skills are essential before starting AP Calculus.

Must-Know for Calculus

  • Unit circle values: Instant recall of sin, cos, tan at standard angles.
  • Identities: Pythagorean, double-angle, and sum/difference formulas.
  • Key limits: lim(x→0) sin x/x = 1 and lim(x→0) (1 − cos x)/x = 0.
  • Derivatives preview: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = −sin x.
  • Trig substitution: For integrals involving √(a² − x²), substitute x = a sin θ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Arithmetic Sequences (nth term, partial sums)
summary_sections.content_html:
Arithmetic Sequences

An arithmetic sequence has a constant common difference d between consecutive terms.

Formulas

  • nth term: a_n = a_1 + (n − 1)d
  • Partial sum (first n terms): S_n = n(a_1 + a_n)/2 = n/2 · [2a_1 + (n − 1)d]

Properties

  • Each term is the average of its two neighbors.
  • Graph of a_n vs n is a straight line with slope d.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Geometric Sequences (nth term, infinite series)
summary_sections.content_html:
Geometric Sequences

A geometric sequence has a constant common ratio r between consecutive terms.

Formulas

  • nth term: a_n = a_1 · r^(n−1)
  • Partial sum: S_n = a_1(1 − rⁿ)/(1 − r), r ≠ 1
  • Infinite sum (|r| < 1): S = a_1/(1 − r)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sigma Notation & Summation Rules
summary_sections.content_html:
Sigma Notation & Summation Rules

Sigma notation (Σ) provides a compact way to write sums.

Notation

  • Σ_{k=1}^{n} a_k = a_1 + a_2 + … + a_n
  • k is the index, 1 is the lower limit, n is the upper limit.

Key Summation Formulas

  • Σ c = cn (constant)
  • Σ k = n(n+1)/2
  • Σ k² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6
  • Σ k³ = [n(n+1)/2]²

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mathematical Induction
summary_sections.content_html:
Mathematical Induction

A proof technique for statements about all natural numbers n ≥ n₀.

Steps

  • Base Case: Verify the statement for n = n₀ (usually n₀ = 1).
  • Inductive Hypothesis: Assume the statement is true for n = k.
  • Inductive Step: Prove it true for n = k + 1 using the hypothesis.

Analogy

  • Like dominoes: if the first falls and each falling domino knocks down the next, all dominoes fall.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Binomial Theorem
summary_sections.content_html:
Binomial Theorem

Expands (a + b)ⁿ into a sum of terms involving binomial coefficients.

Formula

  • (a+b)ⁿ = Σ_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k) a^(n−k) b^k
  • C(n,k) = n! / (k!(n−k)!) — the binomial coefficient, also written "n choose k".

Pascal's Triangle

  • Each entry is the sum of the two entries above.
  • Row n gives the coefficients for (a+b)ⁿ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Finance & Growth Patterns
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Finance & Growth Patterns

Finance

  • Simple interest: arithmetic model (add fixed amount each period).
  • Compound interest: geometric model (multiply by (1 + r/n) each period).
  • Annuities: sum of a geometric series — regular payments with compound growth.
  • Future value of annuity: FV = P·[(1+r)ⁿ − 1]/r.

Growth Patterns

  • Population doubling: a_n = a₀ · 2^(n/T) where T is doubling time.
  • Radioactive decay: geometric with r = (1/2)^(1/T_half).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Probability
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Probability

Sequences, series, and the binomial theorem connect directly to probability and combinatorics.

Binomial Probability

  • P(X = k) = C(n,k) p^k (1−p)^(n−k) for n independent trials with success probability p.

Expected Value & Geometric Distribution

  • Expected number of trials to first success: E[X] = 1/p.
  • Geometric series connects to probability: P(X = k) = (1−p)^(k−1) p.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Concept of Limits (graphical, numerical approaches)
summary_sections.content_html:
Concept of Limits

The limit of f(x) as x approaches c, written lim_{x→c} f(x) = L, means f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L as x gets close to c.

Approaches

  • Graphical: Read the y-value the graph approaches as x → c.
  • Numerical: Make a table of x-values approaching c from both sides.
  • The limit may exist even if f(c) is undefined (e.g., holes).

When Limits Don't Exist

  • Left-hand and right-hand limits disagree.
  • Function oscillates without settling.
  • Function increases/decreases without bound.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Evaluating Limits Algebraically
summary_sections.content_html:
Evaluating Limits Algebraically

Techniques

  • Direct substitution: Try plugging in c first.
  • Factoring: If 0/0, factor and cancel common factors.
  • Rationalizing: Multiply by conjugate for radical expressions.
  • Common factor: Simplify complex fractions.

Limit Laws

  • Sum: lim(f+g) = lim f + lim g
  • Product: lim(f·g) = lim f · lim g
  • Quotient: lim(f/g) = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: One-Sided Limits
summary_sections.content_html:
One-Sided Limits

One-sided limits consider the behavior of f(x) as x approaches c from one direction only.

Notation

  • lim_{x→c⁻} f(x): approach from the left (x < c).
  • lim_{x→c⁺} f(x): approach from the right (x > c).

Key Principle

  • lim_{x→c} f(x) exists ⟺ lim_{x→c⁻} f(x) = lim_{x→c⁺} f(x).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Continuity & Types of Discontinuities
summary_sections.content_html:
Continuity & Discontinuities

Continuity at a Point

  • f is continuous at c if: (1) f(c) is defined, (2) lim_{x→c} f(x) exists, (3) lim_{x→c} f(x) = f(c).

Types of Discontinuities

  • Removable (hole): limit exists but f(c) is missing or different.
  • Jump: left and right limits both exist but differ.
  • Infinite: function approaches ±∞ (vertical asymptote).
  • Oscillating: function oscillates without settling (e.g., sin(1/x) at 0).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Infinite Limits & Asymptotic Behavior
summary_sections.content_html:
Infinite Limits & Asymptotic Behavior

Infinite Limits

  • lim_{x→c} f(x) = ∞ means f(x) grows without bound near c.
  • Corresponds to a vertical asymptote at x = c.

Limits at Infinity

  • lim_{x→∞} f(x) = L means f(x) → L as x grows large.
  • Corresponds to a horizontal asymptote y = L.

End Behavior of Rationals

  • degree(num) < degree(denom): HA at y = 0.
  • degree(num) = degree(denom): HA at y = ratio of leading coefficients.
  • degree(num) > degree(denom): no HA (oblique or grows without bound).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications: Instantaneous Rate of Change
summary_sections.content_html:
Instantaneous Rate of Change

The instantaneous rate of change of f at x = a is the limit of the average rate of change over shrinking intervals.

Formula

  • Instantaneous rate = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h) − f(a)] / h
  • This equals the derivative f'(a) — the slope of the tangent line at x = a.

Average vs Instantaneous

  • Average rate = Δy/Δx = [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a) (secant line).
  • Instantaneous rate = limit of average rate as interval shrinks to zero (tangent line).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Piecewise Functions & Limits
summary_sections.content_html:
Piecewise Functions & Limits

A piecewise function is defined by different formulas on different intervals.

Evaluating Limits

  • At a boundary point c between pieces, compute both one-sided limits.
  • lim_{x→c⁻}: use the formula for x < c.
  • lim_{x→c⁺}: use the formula for x > c (or x ≥ c).
  • Limit exists only if both one-sided limits agree.

Making Piecewise Functions Continuous

  • Choose parameters so that the pieces 'meet' at boundary points.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Calculus Prep
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Calculus Prep: Limits & Continuity

Limits and continuity form the foundation of AP Calculus AB/BC. Mastery of these precalculus skills is essential.

AP-Essential Skills

  • Evaluate limits (direct substitution, factoring, rationalizing, squeeze theorem).
  • Analyze one-sided limits and determine when the two-sided limit exists.
  • Classify discontinuities (removable, jump, infinite).
  • Apply the Intermediate Value Theorem.
  • Know that continuity implies integrability and is needed for the Mean Value Theorem.
  • Understand lim sin x/x = 1 and lim (1−cos x)/x = 0.

u10_l10.1.title: Derivative as Slope of Tangent Line

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Derivative as Slope of Tangent Line
summary_sections.content_html:
Derivative as Slope of Tangent Line

The derivative of f at x = a is defined as the limit of the difference quotient:

  • f'(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h) − f(a)] / h

Geometric Interpretation

  • f'(a) = slope of the tangent line to y = f(x) at x = a.
  • The tangent line is the best linear approximation of f near x = a.
  • Equation: y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Derivative Rules (power rule basics)
summary_sections.content_html:
Derivative Rules

Basic Rules

  • Constant Rule: d/dx[c] = 0
  • Power Rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹
  • Constant Multiple: d/dx[cf(x)] = c · f'(x)
  • Sum/Difference: d/dx[f ± g] = f' ± g'

Common Derivatives

  • d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = −sin x
  • d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ, d/dx[ln x] = 1/x

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Optimization
summary_sections.content_html:
Optimization with Derivatives

Derivatives help find maximum and minimum values of functions.

Process

  • 1. Find f'(x) and set f'(x) = 0 to find critical points.
  • 2. Use the first derivative test (sign changes) or second derivative test (f''(x) > 0 for min, < 0 for max).
  • 3. On a closed interval, also check endpoints.

Real-World Optimization

  • Maximize profit/area/volume or minimize cost/distance.
  • Write a function for the quantity to optimize, set its derivative to zero.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Area Under Curves (intro to integration)
summary_sections.content_html:
Area Under Curves

The area under a curve y = f(x) from x = a to x = b is found using integration.

Riemann Sums

  • Divide [a, b] into n subintervals of width Δx = (b−a)/n.
  • Approximate area: Σ f(x_i) · Δx.
  • As n → ∞, the Riemann sum → definite integral ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx.

Key Idea

  • Integration is the 'reverse' of differentiation.
  • The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects derivatives and integrals.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Connecting Precalculus to Calculus
summary_sections.content_html:
Connecting Precalculus to Calculus

Every major precalculus topic directly supports calculus concepts.

Connections

  • Functions & graphs: Needed for reading and interpreting derivative/integral graphs.
  • Polynomial/rational functions: Easiest functions for differentiation and integration.
  • Trigonometry: Trig functions and identities are used constantly in calculus.
  • Exponentials & logs: Model growth/decay; have elegant derivatives.
  • Limits: Define derivatives and integrals.
  • Sequences & series: Lead to Taylor series, the backbone of approximation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP-Style Practice Problems
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Practice Problems

These problems mirror the format and difficulty of AP Calculus AB free-response and multiple-choice questions, drawing on precalculus skills.

Focus Areas

  • Limits and continuity
  • Derivatives and rates of change
  • Function analysis (increasing/decreasing, concavity)
  • Trig and exponential functions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capstone Project: Modeling with Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Capstone Project: Modeling with Functions

This capstone integrates all precalculus skills: building a mathematical model from data, analyzing it, and making predictions.

Modeling Process

  • 1. Collect/identify real-world data or a scenario.
  • 2. Choose an appropriate function type (linear, quadratic, exponential, trig, logistic).
  • 3. Fit the model to data (regression or algebraic methods).
  • 4. Analyze: find key features (max, min, zeros, asymptotes, rates of change).
  • 5. Predict and validate: use the model for forecasting, check reasonableness.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comprehensive Review & AP Exam Prep
summary_sections.content_html:
Comprehensive Review & AP Exam Prep

A final review covering all major precalculus topics needed for AP Calculus success.

Key Topics to Master

  • Function types: polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric
  • Transformations, compositions, and inverses
  • Limits, continuity, and asymptotic behavior
  • Sequences, series, and the binomial theorem
  • Trig identities and equations
  • Derivatives and integrals (preview)
